I know Mrs. Mallard had heart trouble. People took great care to tell her about her husband’s death. Her sister Josephine told her with broken sentences and hints. Her husband’s friend Richards was also there, close to her. He was in the newspaper office when they got news of the train crash. Brently Mallard's name was at the top of the list of people who died. He sent a second telegram to check if it was true. He wanted to tell his friend the sad news first. She did not hear the story like many women do. They cannot accept its meaning. She cried suddenly in her sister's arms. When her sadness was gone, she went to her room alone. She does not want anyone to follow her. There was a big, soft armchair by the open window. She sank into this. She felt very tired. This tiredness was in her body and her mind. She sees the tops of trees in the square by her house. The trees move with new life in spring. The sweet smell of rain is in the air. A man was shouting to sell his goods in the street. She hears a soft song from far away, and many sparrows sing nearby. There are small parts of blue sky between the clouds. The clouds are high and thick in the west by her window. She sat back on the chair and did not move. She felt sad and sometimes cried softly. It was like a child who cries in its sleep. She is young and has a calm face. Her face shows control and some strength. But now her eyes looked dull and stared at the blue sky. It was not a look of thinking, but showed a stop of smart ideas. Something is coming to her, and she waits for it with fear. What was that? She did not know what it was. It was too small and hard to see. But she felt something coming from the sky. It reached for her with sounds, smells, and colors in the air. Now her chest moved up and down quickly. She starts to see the thing coming for her. She tries to push it away with her mind. Her two white hands feel weak against it. When she let go, a soft word came from her lips. She said it many times quietly: "free, free, free!" Her eyes had a blank look and showed fear. They stayed happy and full of energy. Her heart beats fast, and the warm blood relaxes her body. She did not stop to ask if it was a big joy. She understood well and thought the idea was not important. She knew she would cry again. She saw the kind hands in death. The face looked at her with love. Now it was still, gray, and dead. But she saw a long time ahead that would be hers. She opened her arms and welcomed them. She has no one to live for in the coming years. She will live for herself. No strong will can change hers when people think they can control others. A good or bad intention does not change the crime. She sees this in a short moment. But sometimes she loved him. She often did not have it. "What does it matter?" What does love mean when she feels strong inside? It is free! My body and soul are free! She kept talking softly. Josephine knelt by the closed door. She put her lips to the keyhole. She begged to be let in. "Louise, please open the door." Please open the door. You will get sick. "What are you doing, Louise?" Please open the door now. "Please go away." I do not make myself sick. No, she drinks a special drink through that open window. Her imagination is very active about the days to come. Spring days and summer days are special days for her. She said a quick prayer for a long life. She thought yesterday that life might be long. She got up and opened the door for her sister. She had a strong joy in her eyes. She walked like a winner. She held her sister's waist, and they went down the stairs. Richards waited for them at the bottom. Someone is opening the front door with a key. Brently Mallard came in. He looked a little dirty. He carried his bag and umbrella. He was far from the accident and did not know it happened. He stood amazed at Josephine's loud cry. Richard moved quickly to hide him from his wife. Richards came too late. When the doctors came, they said she died of heart disease. They said it was the joy that kills.
